section.title: Josiah's Reforms
section.sectionRationale: Josiah publicly renews the covenant, then leads the most thorough purge of idolatry in either kingdom's history, destroying every high place, altar, and object of false worship from Jerusalem to former Israelite territory, and restores a Passover unmatched since the days of the judges. Yet the LORD's fierce anger over Manasseh's sins remains unquenched, and Josiah himself dies in battle against Pharaoh Neco, after which Judah's remaining kings quickly resume evil.
section.thesis: Josiah's unparalleled zeal in tearing down idolatry and restoring true worship is the most complete reformation Judah ever saw, yet even this could not turn back the judgment Manasseh's sins had already fixed, showing that no human reform, however thorough, can substitute for the perfect obedience only Christ could offer.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the public covenant renewal, verses 1 to 3; the systematic destruction of idolatry, verses 4 to 20; the restored Passover, verses 21 to 25; and the LORD's unrelenting anger and Josiah's death, verses 26 to 37.

units.label: Covenant renewal
units.note: all the people entered into the covenant

verses.text: Then the king summoned all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.

And he went up to the house of the LORD with all the people of Judah and Jerusalem, as well as the priests and the prophets, all the people small and great, and in their hearing he read all the words of the Book of the Covenant that had been found in the house of the LORD.

So the king stood by the pillar and made a {{berith|covenant}} before the LORD to follow the LORD and to keep His commandments, decrees, and statutes with all his {{lev|heart}} and all his soul, and to carry out the words of the covenant that were written in this book. And all the people entered into the covenant.

Then the king commanded Hilkiah the high priest, the priests second in rank, and the doorkeepers to remove from the temple of the LORD all the articles made for Baal, Asherah, and all the host of heaven. And he burned them outside Jerusalem in the fields of Kidron and carried their ashes to Bethel.

Josiah also did away with the idolatrous priests ordained by the kings of Judah to burn incense on the high places of the cities of Judah and in the places all around Jerusalem, those who had burned incense to Baal, to the sun and moon, to the constellations, and to all the host of heaven.
He brought the Asherah pole from the house of the LORD to the Kidron Valley outside Jerusalem, and there he burned it, ground it to powder, and threw its dust on the graves of the common people.
He also tore down the quarters of the male shrine prostitutes that were in the house of the LORD, where the women had woven tapestries for Asherah.

Then Josiah brought all the priests from the cities of Judah and {{tame|desecrated}} the high places, from Geba to Beersheba, where the priests had burned incense. He tore down the high places of the gates at the entrance of the gate of Joshua the governor of the city, which was to the left of the city gate.
Although the priests of the high places did not come up to the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem, they ate unleavened bread with their fellow priests.
He also desecrated Topheth in the Valley of Ben-hinnom so that no one could sacrifice his son or daughter in the fire to Molech.

And he removed from the entrance to the house of the LORD the horses that the kings of Judah had dedicated to the sun. They were in the court near the chamber of an official named Nathan-melech. And Josiah burned up the chariots of the sun.

He pulled down the altars that the kings of Judah had set up on the roof near the upper chamber of Ahaz, and the altars that Manasseh had set up in the two courtyards of the house of the LORD. The king pulverized them there and threw their dust into the Kidron Valley.

The king also desecrated the high places east of Jerusalem, to the south of the Mount of Corruption, which King Solomon of Israel had built for Ashtoreth the abomination of the Sidonians, for Chemosh the abomination of the Moabites, and for Milcom the abomination of the Ammonites.
He smashed the sacred pillars to pieces, cut down the Asherah poles, and covered the sites with human bones.
He even pulled down the altar at Bethel, the high place set up by Jeroboam son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin. Then he burned the high place, ground it to powder, and burned the Asherah pole.

And as Josiah turned, he saw the tombs there on the hillside, and he sent someone to take the bones out of the tombs, and he burned them on the altar to defile it, according to the word of the LORD proclaimed by the man of God who had foretold these things.

Then the king asked, "What is this monument I see?" And the men of the city replied, "It is the tomb of the man of God who came from Judah and proclaimed these things that you have done to the altar of Bethel."
"Let him rest," said Josiah. "Do not let anyone disturb his bones." So they left his bones undisturbed, along with those of the prophet who had come from Samaria.
Just as Josiah had done at Bethel, so also in the cities of Samaria he removed all the shrines of the high places set up by the kings of Israel who had provoked the LORD to anger.
On the altars he slaughtered all the priests of the high places, and he burned human bones on them. Then he returned to Jerusalem.

units.label: The restored Passover
units.note: no such Passover had been observed

verses.text: The king commanded all the people, "Keep the {{pesach|Passover}} of the LORD your God, as it is written in this Book of the Covenant."
No such Passover had been observed from the days of the judges who had governed Israel through all the days of the kings of Israel and Judah.
But in the eighteenth year of Josiah's reign, this Passover was observed to the LORD in Jerusalem.

Furthermore, Josiah removed the mediums and spiritists, the household gods and idols, and all the abominations that were seen in the land of Judah and in Jerusalem. He did this to carry out the words of the law written in the book that Hilkiah the priest had found in the house of the LORD.
Neither before nor after Josiah was there any king like him, who turned to the LORD with all his heart and with all his soul and with all his strength, according to all the Law of Moses.

units.label: The LORD's unrelenting anger and Josiah's death

verses.text: Nevertheless, the LORD did not turn away from the fury of His burning anger, which was kindled against Judah because of all that Manasseh had done to provoke Him to anger.
For the LORD had said, "I will remove Judah from My sight, just as I removed Israel. I will reject this city Jerusalem, which I chose, and the temple of which I said, 'My Name shall be there.'"
As for the rest of the acts of Josiah and all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
During Josiah's reign, Pharaoh Neco king of Egypt marched up to help the king of Assyria at the Euphrates River. King Josiah went out to confront him, but Neco faced him and killed him at Megiddo.

From Megiddo his servants carried his body in a chariot, brought him to Jerusalem, and buried him in his own tomb. Then the people of the land took Jehoahaz son of Josiah, anointed him, and made him king in place of his father.
Jehoahaz was twenty-three years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem three months. His mother's name was Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah; she was from Libnah.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as his fathers had done.
And Pharaoh Neco imprisoned Jehoahaz at Riblah in the land of Hamath so that he could not reign in Jerusalem, and he imposed on Judah a levy of a hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold.
Then Pharaoh Neco made Eliakim son of Josiah king in place of his father Josiah, and he changed Eliakim's name to Jehoiakim. But Neco took Jehoahaz and carried him off to Egypt, where he died.
So Jehoiakim paid the silver and gold to Pharaoh Neco, but to meet Pharaoh's demand he taxed the land and exacted the silver and the gold from the people, each according to his wealth.
Jehoiakim was twenty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem eleven years. His mother's name was Zebidah daughter of Pedaiah; she was from Rumah.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as his fathers had done.

berith.gloss: Covenant. Josiah publicly renews the berith with the LORD before all the people, echoing similar covenant renewal ceremonies at key moments in Israel's history, Joshua 24, 2 Kings 11, an act meant to formally recommit the entire nation, not just the king, to exclusive covenant loyalty.

tame.gloss: To defile or make unclean. Josiah deliberately makes the high places tame, unfit for any future worship, by covering them with human bones, the ultimate ritual desecration, ensuring these sites could never again be used for illegitimate sacrifice.

pesach.gloss: Passover, Israel's foundational festival commemorating deliverance from Egypt, Exodus 12. That no such Passover had been kept since the days of the judges shows how thoroughly this central covenant celebration had lapsed across the entire monarchy period until Josiah's reforms restored it.

lev.gloss: Heart. The unparalleled commendation that Josiah turned to the LORD with all his lev and soul and strength directly echoes the Shema, Deuteronomy 6:5, marking his devotion as the closest any king came to fulfilling Israel's foundational covenant command in its fullest sense.

basic.context: Following the discovery of the Book of the Law, Josiah publicly renews Judah's covenant with the LORD before the entire assembled nation, then leads history's most thorough purge of idolatry, destroying every altar, high place, and object of false worship from Jerusalem itself to the former northern territories, even desecrating Bethel's altar exactly as an unnamed prophet had foretold centuries earlier. He restores Israel's neglected Passover celebration on a scale unmatched since the days of the judges, and the narrator gives him the highest possible commendation among all of Judah's kings. Yet the LORD's judgment on Judah for Manasseh's earlier sins remains fixed and unrelenting, and Josiah himself dies in battle against Pharaoh Neco at Megiddo, after which his sons Jehoahaz and Jehoiakim quickly resume the evil their father had worked so hard to remove.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing the most extensive religious reformation in either kingdom's history and its immediate aftermath; it is not a promise that thorough reform by one generation guarantees lasting national change or personal safety for the reformer. What endures is the pattern that God delights in wholehearted, comprehensive obedience even when it cannot reverse consequences already fixed by prior unrepented sin, and the sober reality that reform, however genuine, does not automatically transfer to the next generation.
basic.originalLanguages: Josiah renews Judah's berith, covenant, publicly before the whole nation, then works to tame, defile or desecrate, every high place so thoroughly that none could ever be used again. He restores the pesach, Passover, on a scale unmatched since the judges, and the narrator's highest praise is that he turned to the LORD with all his lev, heart, echoing the Shema's own call to total devotion. Yet even this could not avert the judgment already fixed by Manasseh's sins.
basic.typology: Josiah's unparalleled, wholehearted obedience, which still could not avert judgment already provoked by prior generations, previews humanity's need for a righteousness greater than even the best human reformer can offer, a righteousness found only in Christ, whose perfect obedience alone fully satisfies what the Law requires.
basic.christ: Josiah's restoration of the Passover, Israel's central celebration of deliverance from bondage, anticipates the true Passover Lamb, Christ Himself, whose sacrifice accomplishes a far greater and permanent deliverance from bondage to sin, 1 Corinthians 5:7. And Josiah's unmatched wholehearted devotion, described in language echoing the Shema's call to love God with all heart, soul, and strength, points to the perfect fulfillment of that command only realized in Christ, who alone loved the Father completely and without any lapse, succeeding where even history's most zealous reformer ultimately could not avert judgment.
basic.god: God delights in comprehensive, wholehearted reform and gives Josiah the highest possible commendation for it, showing that genuine obedience matters deeply to Him even when it cannot undo every consequence of prior sin. Yet God's justice remains firm; the judgment provoked by Manasseh's generation-spanning unfaithfulness proceeds despite Josiah's remarkable personal righteousness, showing that corporate consequences of sin can outlast even the most thorough individual or single-generation reform.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 13:1-2 records the unnamed prophet's specific, centuries-earlier prediction that a king named Josiah would desecrate Bethel's altar exactly as narrated here, showing God's sovereign foreknowledge across generations. Deuteronomy 6:4-5 gives the Shema's call to love the LORD with all heart, soul, and strength, the very standard Josiah is commended for meeting more fully than any other king. 1 Corinthians 5:7 identifies Christ as the true Passover Lamb, the ultimate fulfillment of the festival Josiah restores here.
basic.oneStory: Josiah's unparalleled, wholehearted reformation represents the closest any king of Judah came to fulfilling the covenant's total demand for devotion, yet even this could not reverse the judgment Manasseh's generation had already fixed, showing that no human reform, however thorough and sincere, can substitute for the perfect obedience only Christ, the true Passover Lamb, could ultimately provide.

context.literary: The chapter's structure moves from public covenant ceremony, to an exhaustive, almost inventory-like catalogue of destroyed idolatrous sites stretching across two kingdoms' worth of territory, to the joyful restoration of Passover, before ending abruptly and jarringly with continued divine judgment and Josiah's own violent death, refusing readers any simple, uncomplicated triumphant conclusion.
context.historical: The specific idolatrous sites destroyed, associated with Baal, Asherah, Molech, Ashtoreth, Chemosh, and Milcom, represent centuries of accumulated Canaanite and regional pagan worship dating back even to Solomon's own reign, 1 Kings 11:7, showing the sheer scale of accumulated compromise Josiah confronted. Pharaoh Neco's campaign to assist Assyria against the rising Babylonian empire reflects the genuine and shifting geopolitical alliances of this era, as Egypt sought to prevent total Babylonian dominance.
context.canonical: Josiah's desecration of Bethel's altar fulfills, in remarkable and specific detail, the prophecy given by an unnamed man of God centuries earlier in 1 Kings 13:1-2, one of the most striking examples of long-range prophetic fulfillment in the entire Old Testament, while his death at Megiddo and the rapid moral decline of his successors set the stage for the final chapters leading to Jerusalem's destruction and exile.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Josiah's unparalleled, comprehensive religious reformation as the highest expression of covenant faithfulness among Judah's kings, while showing that even this remarkable obedience could not reverse the judgment already fixed by Manasseh's earlier, generation-spanning sins.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical reformation and its complicated aftermath; it does not prescribe that thorough religious reform guarantees personal safety, as Josiah's own death in battle immediately demonstrates, nor does it suggest reform is therefore futile, since the narrator's commendation of Josiah remains unqualified and the reforms themselves were genuinely valuable and pleasing to God.

debates.question: Why did Josiah die in battle against Pharaoh Neco despite his unparalleled faithfulness, and was this itself a form of judgment?

views.label: An unwise, unnecessary military intervention rather than direct divine judgment on Josiah personally
views.case: 2 Chronicles 35:20-24 records that Neco explicitly warned Josiah he had no quarrel with Judah and was not sent by Josiah's own God to fight him, suggesting Josiah's death resulted from his own decision to intervene in a conflict not his own, rather than being a specific divine punishment for personal sin, distinct from the separate, already-declared judgment on the nation for Manasseh's sins.

views.label: Part of the broader unfolding of the judgment already declared against Judah
views.case: Given that this chapter explicitly states the LORD's anger against Judah for Manasseh's sins remained unrelenting immediately before recounting Josiah's death, some read his death as itself an early manifestation of that unfolding national judgment, removing Judah's best defense, its most faithful king, just as instability and foreign domination begin to accelerate toward the exile.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Josiah's death, whatever its precise cause, occurs within the framework of a judgment against Judah already fixed because of Manasseh, and neither view diminishes the narrator's unqualified praise of Josiah's own personal righteousness and reforms.

study.originalLanguages: Josiah leads all Judah in publicly renewing the berith, covenant, before the LORD, then works to tame, defile, every high place so completely that none could ever again host illegitimate worship. He restores the pesach, Passover, unmatched since the era of the judges, and receives the narrator's highest possible praise for turning to the LORD with all his lev, heart, language deliberately echoing the Shema's total demand for devotion, Deuteronomy 6:5. Yet even this unmatched, wholehearted obedience could not reverse the judgment already fixed by Manasseh's earlier sins.

typology.type: A restored Passover celebrating deliverance from bondage, unmatched in scale for generations
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Passover Lamb, whose sacrifice accomplishes complete and lasting deliverance from bondage to sin, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

typology.type: Unparalleled wholehearted devotion that still could not avert judgment already fixed by prior sin
typology.fulfillment: The perfect obedience of Christ alone, which fully satisfies the Law's demand and averts the judgment human sin deserves, Romans 5:19.

study.christ: Josiah's restoration of Israel's Passover, its foundational celebration of deliverance from bondage in Egypt, anticipates Christ, the true Passover Lamb, whose sacrifice accomplishes a far greater and truly lasting deliverance from bondage to sin itself, 1 Corinthians 5:7. And Josiah's unmatched devotion, turning to the LORD with all his heart, soul, and strength in direct fulfillment of the Shema's total demand, Deuteronomy 6:5, still could not reverse judgment already provoked by a prior generation's sin, pointing forward to the truth that only Christ's perfect obedience, not even history's most zealous human reformer, can fully satisfy what the Law requires and avert the judgment sin deserves, Romans 5:19.
study.god: God delights in comprehensive, wholehearted reform and gives Josiah history's highest commendation among Judah's kings for it, showing that genuine, thorough obedience matters deeply to Him regardless of its ultimate ability to reverse every prior consequence. Yet God's justice remains unwavering; the judgment provoked by Manasseh's generation-spanning unfaithfulness proceeds despite Josiah's remarkable personal righteousness, demonstrating that the corporate consequences of persistent, unrepented sin can outlast even the most sincere and thorough reform led by a single faithful generation.

crossReferences.note: Records an unnamed prophet's centuries-earlier prediction that a king named Josiah would desecrate Bethel's altar, fulfilled here in remarkable, specific detail.
The Shema's call to love the LORD with all heart, soul, and strength, the exact standard Josiah is commended for meeting more fully than any other king.
Gives additional detail on Josiah's death at Megiddo, including Pharaoh Neco's warning that he had not been sent to fight Judah.
Identifies Christ as the true Passover Lamb, the ultimate and lasting fulfillment of the festival Josiah restores in this chapter.

study.oneStory: Josiah's unparalleled, wholehearted reformation represents the fullest expression of covenant devotion among all of Judah's kings, yet even this remarkable obedience could not reverse the judgment Manasseh's generation had already fixed, and his own untimely death in battle underscores that no human reform, however thorough and sincere, can finally substitute for the perfect obedience only Christ, the true Passover Lamb, was able to provide.

quiz.q: What public act did Josiah lead before beginning his extensive purge of idolatry?

options.text: He publicly read the Book of the Covenant to the assembled people and led them in renewing the covenant with the LORD.
options.note: Verses 1 to 3 record this exact public covenant renewal ceremony.

options.text: He immediately executed all the idolatrous priests without any prior ceremony.
options.note: A public covenant renewal preceded the destruction of idolatry and its associated priests, described later in the chapter.

options.text: He simply issued a private royal decree with no public gathering.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a public assembly of elders and all the people, not a private decree alone.

options.text: No specific ceremony or public act is described in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 1 to 3 give a detailed account of this specific public covenant renewal.

quiz.q: How extensive was Josiah's destruction of idolatrous worship sites?

options.text: It extended from Jerusalem's own temple to high places throughout Judah and even into the former northern territory, including Bethel and the cities of Samaria.
options.note: Verses 4 to 20 describe this remarkably comprehensive geographic scope of reform.

options.text: It was limited only to the temple in Jerusalem itself.
options.note: The reforms extended far beyond the temple, reaching throughout Judah and into former Israelite territory.

options.text: He removed only the most recently built idolatrous altars.
options.note: The reforms included ancient sites dating back even to Solomon's reign, not merely recent additions.

options.text: No specific description of the scope of destruction is given.
options.note: Verses 4 to 20 give an extensive, detailed catalogue of destroyed sites across a wide geographic area.

quiz.q: How is the Passover Josiah restored described in comparison to prior celebrations?

options.text: No such Passover had been observed from the days of the judges through all the days of the kings of Israel and Judah.
options.note: Verse 22 gives this exact, striking comparison highlighting the celebration's unmatched scale.

options.text: It was a smaller celebration than those held under previous kings.
options.note: The text explicitly describes it as unmatched in scale since the era of the judges, not smaller than recent celebrations.

options.text: Passover had been celebrated faithfully every year throughout the monarchy.
options.note: The text explicitly states this specific celebration was unprecedented, implying significant prior neglect.

options.text: No comparison to previous Passover celebrations is given.
options.note: Verse 22 explicitly and specifically makes this striking historical comparison.

quiz.q: Despite Josiah's reforms, what did the LORD determine regarding Judah's fate?

options.text: The LORD's burning anger against Judah, kindled by Manasseh's sins, did not turn away, and Judah would still be removed from His sight as Israel had been.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 record this exact, unrelenting judgment despite Josiah's reforms.

options.text: Judah's judgment was completely canceled because of Josiah's faithfulness.
options.note: The text explicitly states the LORD's anger did not turn away, the opposite of a canceled judgment.

options.text: Only Israel, not Judah, remained under any threat of judgment.
options.note: The text explicitly states Judah itself would be removed from the LORD's sight just as Israel had been.

options.text: No mention of ongoing judgment against Judah is given in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 explicitly and directly address the continuing judgment against Judah.

quiz.q: How did Josiah die, and what happened to Judah's kingship immediately afterward?

options.text: He was killed in battle by Pharaoh Neco at Megiddo, and after a brief reign by his son Jehoahaz, Neco installed Jehoiakim, who, like Jehoahaz, did evil in the LORD's sight.
options.note: Verses 29 to 37 record this exact sequence of Josiah's death and his sons' quickly resumed evil.

options.text: He died peacefully of old age after a long and secure reign.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a violent death in battle, not a peaceful death of old age.

options.text: His successors continued his reforms faithfully without any regression.
options.note: The text explicitly states both Jehoahaz and Jehoiakim did evil in the LORD's sight, a clear regression from Josiah's reforms.

options.text: Judah had no king after Josiah's death.
options.note: The text explicitly names Jehoahaz and then Jehoiakim as his immediate successors.

questions: Josiah led the most thorough reformation in either kingdom's history, yet even this could not reverse Judah's coming judgment. Where might you need to accept that some consequences of past sin, even after genuine repentance, will still play out?

Josiah's reforms reached from Jerusalem's own temple to distant, long-established idolatrous sites throughout the land. What area of your own life, perhaps a long-standing habit or belief, needs the same kind of thorough, comprehensive examination rather than a partial fix?

The Passover Josiah restored had been neglected for generations before he revived it. What spiritual practice or celebration in your own life has been quietly neglected that you sense God calling you to restore?

Josiah is commended for turning to the LORD with all his heart, soul, and strength, the fullest possible devotion. Where in your own life is God calling you toward a more wholehearted, rather than partial, obedience?

Josiah's sons quickly abandoned the reforms he had worked so hard to establish. What practices are you putting in place to help the faith or values you hold dear take root in the next generation rather than simply depending on your own personal example?

translationNotes.note: The reference to high places from Geba to Beersheba spans the full traditional north-south extent of Judah's territory, emphasizing the comprehensive, nationwide scope of Josiah's reforms.

This desecration of Bethel's altar fulfills, in remarkable specific detail, the prophecy of the unnamed man of God given roughly three centuries earlier in 1 Kings 13:1-2, one of Scripture's most striking examples of long-range prophetic fulfillment.

2 Chronicles 35:20-24 provides additional detail on this battle, including Pharaoh Neco's explicit warning that he had no quarrel with Josiah and was not sent by God to fight against him, information not repeated in this account.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 23:1-37. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
